sections.title: Inversions with Negative Adverbial Words and Phrases

content.subtitle: Hardly / Scarcely / Barely ... when
examples: Hardly had the new law been introduced when the mistake was realised.
Scarcely had I opened the front door when I heard a noise from the kitchen.
Barely had we solved one problem when another one arose.

content.subtitle: No sooner ... than
examples: No sooner had the alarm gone off than the police arrived.

content.subtitle: Only + adverb / prepositional phrase
examples: Only in an emergency should you dial 999.
Only after I had checked that the burglars had left did I call the police.
Only when we agree what measures are needed will we be able to solve the problem.

content.subtitle: Not until
examples: Not until the next election will we know how the public feel about this news.

content.subtitle: Other negative adverbials
items: At no time/point/stage: At no point did I realise that he was the Prime Minister.
In no way: In no way does this decision represent a change in government policy.
Little: Little did Ralph know that the burglar was still inside his house.
Never: Never have I heard such a ridiculous suggestion!
Not: Not one vote did the proposal receive.
Not only (… but also/too): Not only has this government failed but it has also stolen ideas from other parties.
On no account: On no account should you try to tackle a burglar yourself.
Rarely: Rarely do the newspapers present a balanced view of current events.
Seldom: Seldom do people leaving prison stay out of trouble.
Under no circumstances: Under no circumstances will we accept an increase in working hours.

sections.title: Inversions with Adverbial Expressions of Place

content.subtitle: Here / There
examples: Here comes the Minister now.
There stood the next king of England.

content.subtitle: Adverbial phrases
examples: At the top of society are the aristocracy.
Beside the Town Hall stood the public library.
In this prison are housed some of the most dangerous criminals.
On the corner of the street sat a homeless man.

content.subtitle: Participle phrases
examples: Running down the road was a young man with a woman's handbag under his arm.

content.subtitle: In short answers
examples: 'I voted for Smith.' 'Did you? So did I.'
'I don't believe a word this government says.' 'No, neither do I.'

content.subtitle: After as, than, so and such
examples: I am very worried about bullying in the school, as are a lot of the parents.
The police in this area make more arrests than do officers in other parts of the country.
So rare is burglary here that many people don't bother to lock their doors.
Such public interest was there in the story that it was on the front pages of the newspapers.

content.subtitle: In conditional sentences
examples: Were the Foreign Secretary to resign, it would cause serious problems for the Prime Minister.
Should the Foreign Secretary resign, it would cause serious problems for the Prime Minister.
Had I known about the crime problem, I would never have moved here.

content.subtitle: all (that)
examples: All that Keith wanted was to get his money back.

content.subtitle: It is/was … who/which/that
examples: It was Carol who/that called the police.
It was I who stole the money. (formal)
It was me that stole the money. (informal)

content.subtitle: the … thing
examples: The first thing is to check to see what's missing.

content.subtitle: the day/etc when/that / the day/etc on/in/at which
examples: The year when this government came to power was 2006.

content.subtitle: the person who/that
examples: The person who stole the money was Thomas.

content.subtitle: the place where
examples: The place where the Queen stays in Scotland is Balmoral Castle.

content.subtitle: the reason (why)
examples: The reason (why) I joined this political party was to make a difference.

content.subtitle: the thing that
examples: The thing that annoys me is the boss's attitude.

content.subtitle: what
examples: What annoys me is the boss's attitude.
What Churchill did was bring people together.
What happened was that a witness saw the man leave the house.

content.subtitle: so
examples: It all happened so quickly that I didn't have time to see the man's face.
This problem has gone on for so long that I don't think they'll ever find a solution.
It was so terrible a crime that the judge sentenced him to life in prison.
There is so much crime around here that I'm thinking of moving.

content.subtitle: such
examples: This problem has gone on for such a long time that I don't think they'll ever find a solution.
It was such a terrible crime that the judge sentenced him to life in prison.
There is such a lot of crime around here that I'm thinking of moving.

content.subtitle: too
examples: I had too little time to get a good look at his face.
This problem seems to be too difficult for them to solve.
The police responded too slowly to have any chance of catching the burglar.

content.subtitle: enough
examples: There just aren't enough police officers on the streets.
The police weren't quick enough to catch the burglar.
The police didn't respond quickly enough to catch the burglar.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description: So and such can also be used without a that clause. Too is only used to describe something that is more than necessary with a negative effect. Enough comes before nouns and after adjectives and adverbs.
examples: ✓ There's so much crime around here these days.
✓ Politics is so boring!
✓ You're such a bully!
✗ This problem seems to be too difficult for them to solve it.

sections.title: A: Correcting Inversion Errors
sections.instructions: If a phrase in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: Hardly I had sat down when the doorbell rang.
Scarcely had Julian finished writing when the teacher told the students to put their pens down.
We had barely set off on our journey when the kids started asking when we would get there.
No sooner the government changes the tax laws than businesses find a way around them.
items.answer: No sooner does the government change

items.sentence: Scarcely they had finished painting the house when it started to rain.
Hardly the new computer system had been installed when it started to go wrong.
items.answer: Hardly had the new computer system been installed

items.sentence: It’s true that no sooner we had started eating than we realised we had forgotten the potatoes.
Barely had the politician started to speak when began the crowd to boo.
items.answer: when the crowd began to boo

items.sentence: Hardly the new park was finished when vandals destroyed the flowerbeds.
items.answer: Hardly was the new park finished

items.sentence: Scarcely Keith had arrived in Argentina when he was arrested.

sections.title: B: Completing with Negative Adverbials
sections.instructions: Complete using the words and phrases in the box.

items.sentence: ...... were the security guards to blame for what happened.
...... once did the old woman thank me for helping her.
...... seeing the doctor was Theresa allowed to leave hospital.
...... will passengers be allowed to carry more than 6 kg of hand luggage.
...... were you late, but you also forgot to bring the correct documents.
...... during the meeting did anyone mention that the managing director was planning to leave.
...... did the passengers know that the driver was really an undercover police officer.
...... do you meet anyone as charming as Mr Beeching.
...... should unaccompanied children be allowed to enter the area.
...... before has the company found itself facing such stiff competition.

sections.title: C: Completing Inversions
sections.instructions: Complete using the words in bold with the verb in the correct form.

items.sentence: Not until the area is made safe ...... . (construction / can continue)
In no way ...... (the announcement / affect) how strongly we felt about the situation.
Seldom ...... (you / see) a vintage car in quite such good condition these days.
Not only ...... (Jerry / forget) my birthday, but he also forgot our wedding anniversary.
At no time ...... (I / ask) my opinion on the subject.
Only when I had checked the ticket again did I realise that I had won first prize!
items.answer: I had checked, did I realise

items.sentence: Little did Caroline realise that I had a big surprise planned for her.
Under no circumstances should you sign a contract you haven't read thoroughly.
At no stage did it appear likely that Wilkinson would win the championship.
Only if the weather is clear will the space shuttle be allowed to launch.
items.answer: will the space shuttle be allowed

sections.title: D: Place Inversions
sections.instructions: Complete using a verb from the box in the correct form.

items.sentence: 'There (1) ...... our new neighbour now,' said Joe.
I looked up and coming along the path (2) ...... a short, fat man.
On his head (3) ...... a bright red baseball cap.
From an upstairs window (4) ...... the sound of loud rock music.
In his hand (5) ...... a letter from the council.
There (6) ...... a little old woman, looking up at us, smiling.
On the sofa behind her (7) ...... a fat old cat.
Suddenly, in the doorway (8) ...... the same fat man I had seen earlier.

sections.title: E: Choosing Correct Inversions
sections.instructions: Circle the correct phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: Dave doesn't really trust Ian, and I have to say that neither **I do / do I**.
Such a valuable painting **it is / is it** that the public are not allowed to get close to it.
So **we could / could we** watch TV while we ate, we moved the television onto the balcony.
My dad's never been abroad, and nor **my mum has / has my mum**.
Such **the media interest was / was the media interest** in the wedding that there were over a dozen photographers.
It's been a hard season for the team, as **last season was / was last season**.
Russia is a country with vast natural resources, and so **China is / is China**.
We in this country spend more per person on fuel than **the French do / do the French**.
The Minister was forced to resign, such **the pressure was / was the pressure** on her.
So nervous **he was / was he** that I was almost certain he was lying.

sections.title: F: Cleft Sentences
sections.instructions: Rewrite the sentences, starting with the words given.

items.sentence: The window was broken by a cricket ball.
items.answer: It was a cricket ball that broke the window.

items.sentence: I will never forget the day I heard I'd got into my chosen university.
items.answer: The day on which I heard I'd got into my chosen university is one I'll never forget.

items.sentence: Glynn became a social worker to help people less fortunate than himself.
items.answer: The reason Glynn became a social worker was to help people less fortunate than himself.

items.sentence: Getting through the summer without getting injured was the only thing the athlete wanted.
items.answer: All the athlete wanted was to get through the summer without getting injured.

items.sentence: The driver didn't see the motorcyclist.
items.answer: What happened was that the driver didn't see the motorcyclist.

items.sentence: Christopher Columbus discovered America.
items.answer: The person who discovered America was Christopher Columbus.

items.sentence: Seeing Patricia cry like that made me feel guilty.
items.answer: What made me feel guilty was seeing Patricia cry like that.

items.sentence: What we had to do first was decide where to meet.
items.answer: The first thing we had to do was decide where to meet.

items.sentence: There are lots of facilities where we go camping.
items.answer: The place where we go camping has lots of facilities.

items.sentence: Einstein proved that energy and mass are basically the same thing.
items.answer: What Einstein proved was that energy and mass are basically the same thing.

sections.title: G: so, such, too, enough
sections.instructions: Write so, such, too or enough in each gap.

items.sentence: It was ………………… good a meal that we left the waiter quite a large tip.
It's becoming increasingly clear that there just aren't ………………… people willing to buy our products online.
I'm quite enjoying my maths degree, but I just find statistics ………………… boring!
Angela seemed to be having ………………… a good time at the party that I decided not to tell her how late it was.
We've all been working ………………… hard on the latest project the manager gave us a couple of extra days off.
I worry about my grandparents because they have ………………… a lot of health problems.
It's ………………… early to tell whether the business is going to succeed or not.
Set the alarm or we'll never wake up early ………………… to catch the first train.
I never knew that Andrew had ………………… few friends.
Everyone was surprised when Dan failed because he's ………………… a good student.
A year after losing my job, I had ………………… many debts that I was really beginning to panic.
I was hoping to have a word with Alan before he left, but it seems to be ………………… late now.

sections.title: H: Sentence Transformation
sections.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: I had to clear the spare room before I could start decorating. (cleared)
items.answer: Only when I had cleared the spare room could I start decorating.

items.sentence: The phone rang again as soon as I put it down. (had)
items.answer: Hardly had I put the phone down when it rang again.

items.sentence: The Watsons moved to London and very soon after they decided to get divorced. (had)
items.answer: No sooner had the Watsons moved to London than they decided to get divorced.

items.sentence: I had no idea the manager wanted to see me to fire me. (realise)
items.answer: Little did I realise that the manager wanted to see me to fire me.

items.sentence: I was told about the website by Charlie. (who)
items.answer: It was Charlie who told me about the website.

items.sentence: Buying a plane ticket at the last minute isn't often possible. (to)
items.answer: Rarely is it possible to buy a plane ticket at the last minute.

items.sentence: They didn't ask me anything about my plans for the summer. (question)
items.answer: Not one question did they ask me about my plans for the summer.

items.sentence: There is no situation in which we will allow the management to lower wages. (no)
items.answer: Under no circumstances will we allow the management to lower wages.

items.sentence: Not until we have no choice ………………… the business down.
It was ……………………… a victory that even Smith's fans couldn't believe it.
At no time …………………… I was going to fail the exam.
So ……………………… that they actually finished three weeks early.
Never ……………………… complete and utter rubbish!
In no way ……………………… that people will be prevented from organising peaceful protests.
If you don't put the tent up …………………, it might blow away!
No sooner ……………………… everyone started to gossip about him.
'I can't stand this hot weather!' 'No, ……………………… . Dreadful, isn't it?'
'We were at the Rolling Stones concert last weekend.' 'Were you? So ……………………… !'

sections.title: J: Cloze Text
sections.instructions: Complete the text with one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Rarely (1) ................. neighbours have the best of relationships for any length of time.
Of course, some people become close friends, but only if there is a lot of give and take (2) .................... this possible.
(3) ................... common are disputes between neighbours that there are many laws to cover arguments over noise, property and rubbish.
Before things go (4) .................... far for the relationship to be saved, there are a few simple things you can do.
The first is (5) .................... speak to your neighbour.
You'd be amazed at how many arguments are avoided by (6) .................... a simple step.
Not (7) .................... does it give you the chance to express your complaint, it (8) .................... gives your neighbour the chance to explain and, perhaps, take action.
On (9) .................... account should you do anything to damage your neighbours' property, or their overhanging trees if that's the problem, without knowing what your legal rights are.
(10) .................... until you are sure you are acting within the law should you do anything.
